notes.note: Only a man who had held the praetorship carried the standing to govern a province with imperium; of Cicero's staff only his brother Quintus, an ex-praetor, qualified, which is why the succession was so awkward.

notes.anchor: an annual stipend has been voted
notes.note: The Senate had decreed a year's pay (stipendium) for the province's troops, implying the garrison and the threat of the Parthian/Syrian war would continue past Cicero's term and burden whoever he left in charge.

notes.anchor: a sketch of the whole state of the commonwealth
notes.note: Returning after eighteen months away, Cicero asks Atticus for a written briefing on the deteriorating politics at Rome (the Curio/Paulus manoeuvres against Caesar) so as not to arrive 'a foreigner and a stranger.'

notes.anchor: twelve percent per annum, renewed yearly
notes.note: Cicero's edict allowed only centesimae usurae (one per cent a month) with interest re-contracted each year rather than compounded continuously; Scaptius held out for the bond's far higher rate and so refused payment.
A talent was a Greek unit of account worth roughly 24,000 sesterces; the hundred talents settled for Brutus this year on the Cappadocian debt thus came to some 2.4 million sesterces.

A tribunatus militum, a staff commission in the governor's army; Cicero had granted one to Scaptius at Brutus's request, but Scaptius declined it once denied the cavalry and prefecture he really wanted.
The cibaria praefecti were the supplies and allowances due to a serving prefect, levied on the provincials; Gavius demanded them although Cicero had never employed him, and was rebuffed.
